steps.title: Compute each power
steps.content: Subtract full periods of $2\pi$. Since $\tfrac{565\pi}{6}=\tfrac{564\pi}{6}+\tfrac{\pi}{6}=47\cdot 2\pi+\tfrac{\pi}{6}$, the angle is coterminal with $\tfrac{\pi}{6}$.

steps.title: Add and identify
steps.content: So $$\cos\!\tfrac{565\pi}{6}=\cos\tfrac{\pi}{6}=\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}.$$ That matches choice C — C is correct.

steps.title: Use the source rationale
steps.content: Choice C is correct. The cosine of an angle is equal to the cosine of $n(2π)$ radians more than the angle, where $n$ is an integer constant. Since $\frac{565π}{6}$ is equivalent to $47(2π)+\frac{π}{6}$, $\cos(\frac{565π}{6})$ can be rewritten as $\cos(47(2π)+\frac{π}{6})$, which is equal to $\cos(\frac{π}{6})$. Therefore, the value of $\cos(\frac{565π}{6})$ is equal to the value of $\cos(\frac{π}{6})$, which is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Alternate approach: A trigonometric ratio can be found using the unit circle, that is, a circle with radius $1$ unit. The cosine of a number $t$ is the x-coordinate of the point resulting from traveling a distance of $t$ counterclockwise from the point $(1,0)$ around a unit circle centered at the origin in the xy-plane. A unit circle has a circumference of $2π$. It follows that since $\frac{565π}{6}$ is equal to $47(2π)+\frac{π}{6}$, traveling a distance of $\frac{565π}{6}$ counterclockwise around a unit circle means traveling around the circle completely $47$ times and then another $\frac{π}{6}$ beyond that. That is, traveling $\frac{565π}{6}$ results in the same point as traveling $\frac{π}{6}$. Traveling $\frac{π}{6}$ counterclockwise from the point $(1,0)$ around a unit circle centered at the origin in the xy-plane results in the point $(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2},\frac{1}{2})$. Thus, the value of $\cos\frac{565π}{6}$ is the x-coordinate of the point $(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2},\frac{1}{2})$, which is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice A is incorrect. This is the value of $\sin\frac{565π}{6}$, not $\cos\frac{565π}{6}$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice B is incorrect. This is the value of the cosine of a multiple of $2π$, not $\frac{565π}{6}$.

steps.title: Check the choices
steps.content: Choice D is incorrect. This is the value of $\frac{1}{\tan\frac{565π}{6}}$, not $\cos\frac{565π}{6}$.
